corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: We describe the simple design of LoRA and its practical benefits.
Specifically, we evaluate on the GLUE benchmark for RoBERTa and DeBERTa.

We propose Low-Rank Adaptation, or LoRA, which freezes the pretrained model weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture, greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters for downstream tasks.

We propose Low-Rank Adaptation, or LoRA, which freezes the pretrained model weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture, greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters for downstream tasks.
1 While GPT-3 175B achieves non-trivial performance with few-shot learning, fine-tuning boosts its performance significantly as shown in Appendix A.
As larger models are trained every few months, this changes from a mere "inconvenience" for GPT-2 or RoBERTa large to a critical deployment challenge for with 175 billion trainable parameters.
However, existing techniques often introduce inference latency by extending model depth or reduce the model's usable sequence length (Section 3).

Instead, we only optimize the injected, much smaller low-rank matrices. • Our simple linear design allows us to merge the trainable matrices with the frozen weights when deployed, introducing no inference latency compared to a fully fine-tuned model, by construction. • LoRA is orthogonal to many prior methods and can be combined with many of them, such as prefix-tuning.
Transformer is a sequence-to-sequence architecture that makes heavy use of self-attention. applied it to autoregressive language modeling by using a stack of Transformer decoders.
Since then, Transformer-based language models have dominated NLP, achieving the state-of-the-art in many tasks.

A new paradigm emerged with BERT and GPT-2 ) -both are large Transformer lan-guage models trained on a large amount of text -where fine-tuning on task-specific data after pretraining on general domain data provides a significant performance gain compared to training on task-specific data directly.

A new paradigm emerged with BERT and GPT-2 ) -both are large Transformer lan-guage models trained on a large amount of text -where fine-tuning on task-specific data after pretraining on general domain data provides a significant performance gain compared to training on task-specific data directly.
Fine-tuning retrains a model pre-trained on general domains to a specific task ; .
Similarly, combining LoRA with other tensor product-based methods could potentially improve its parameter efficiency, which we leave to future work.
F.2 ADDITIONAL EXPERIMENTS ON GPT-3 We present additional runs on GPT-3 with different adaptation methods in Table .
We propose LoRA, an efficient adaptation strategy that neither introduces inference latency nor reduces input sequence length while retaining high model quality.
Acc./%) GPT-3 Few-Shot 40.6 69.0 GPT-3 Fine-Tuned 89.5 85.4 Table 8: Fine-tuning significantly outperforms few-shot learning on GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020).
